stem: Substance abuse is characterized by the inability to control or stop substance use despite the negative consequences it causes. The DSM-5, published by which organization, provides the diagnostic criteria for substance use disorders?
options: The American Psychiatric Association, which publishes the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5)
The World Health Organization, which publishes the International Classification of Diseases (ICD-11)
The American Psychological Association, which publishes the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5)

stem: According to DSM-5, to establish a diagnosis of substance use disorder, an individual must meet at least how many criteria within a 12-month period?
options: At least two or more of the 11 outlined criteria within a 12-month period
At least three or more of the 11 outlined criteria within a 12-month period
At least four or more of the 11 outlined criteria within a 12-month period

stem: The severity of substance use disorder is determined by the number of DSM-5 criteria met. Which classification is CORRECT?
options: Mild: 2-3 criteria; Moderate: 4-5 criteria; Severe: 6 or more criteria
Mild: 1-2 criteria; Moderate: 3-4 criteria; Severe: 5 or more criteria
Mild: 2-3 criteria; Moderate: 4-6 criteria; Severe: 7 or more criteria

stem: The FIRST of the four main DSM-5 criteria for establishing a diagnosis of substance dependence is Impaired Control. Which statement MOST accurately describes this criterion?
options: The individual demonstrates difficulty in controlling their substance use â manifested by unsuccessful attempts to cut down or control substance use, persistent desire or unsuccessful efforts to quit, and spending significant time obtaining, using, or recovering from substance use

The individual continues to use the substance despite persistent social or interpersonal problems caused or worsened by its effects, and gives up important social, occupational, or recreational activities
The individual experiences tolerance, needing increased amounts to achieve the desired effect, or experiencing diminished effects with continued use of the same amount

stem: Tolerance is one of the DSM-5 criteria for substance use disorder. Which of the following MOST accurately defines tolerance as described in the text?
options: Needing increased amounts of the substance to achieve the desired effect, or experiencing diminished effects with continued use of the same amount
The presence of withdrawal syndrome or using the substance to relieve or avoid withdrawal symptoms
Taking the substance in larger amounts or over a longer period than intended despite awareness of harm

stem: Withdrawal as a DSM-5 criterion for substance use disorder is characterized by which of the following?
options: The presence of a withdrawal syndrome or using the substance to relieve or avoid withdrawal symptoms
Needing increased amounts of the substance to achieve the desired effect or experiencing diminished effects with continued use
Continued use of the substance despite being aware of its physical or psychological harm to the individual

stem: In the process of becoming drug dependent, the stage where the body develops tolerance and the individual uses more of the substance more frequently to experience the same level of intoxication is called:
options: Increased tolerance â where with regular use the body develops tolerance requiring larger amounts of the substance to achieve desired effects
Psychological and physical dependence â where dependence begins to develop as the individual becomes psychologically and physically reliant on the substance
Regular use â where continued use of the substance leads to regular use as the individual begins to incorporate it into their routine

stem: Psychological dependence is distinguished from physical dependence in the text. Which pair CORRECTLY contrasts these two types of dependence?
options: Psychological dependence involves intense craving and desire for the drug; physical dependence is marked by the occurrence of withdrawal symptoms when the substance is reduced or discontinued
Psychological dependence involves withdrawal symptoms when the substance is reduced; physical dependence is marked by intense craving and desire for the drug
Psychological dependence involves needing increased amounts for the same effect; physical dependence involves continued use despite social and occupational consequences

stem: Phase I of alcohol dependence is called the Pre-Alcoholic Phase. Which of the following MOST accurately describes what characterises this phase?
options: They may start to rely on alcohol as a coping mechanism for stress or emotional issues; it is characterized by the use of alcohol to relieve everyday stress and tensions of life; as this continues daily, tolerance develops and the amount required to achieve the desired effect increases

This begins with blackouts â brief periods of amnesia that occur during or immediately following a period of taking the alcohol; alcohol is no longer a source of pleasure or relief but rather a drug required by the individual

Individuals become physically and psychologically dependent on alcohol; they experience intense cravings and may prioritise alcohol over other aspects of their lives; loss of control is described as the inability to choose whether or not to drink

stem: Phase II of alcohol dependence is called the Early Alcohol Phase. Which feature specifically distinguishes Phase II from Phase I?
options: Phase II begins with blackouts â brief periods of amnesia during or immediately following a period of taking alcohol; alcohol is no longer a source of pleasure or relief but rather a drug required by the individual
Phase II is characterized by individuals becoming physically and psychologically dependent on alcohol, experiencing intense cravings and prioritising alcohol over all other aspects of life

Phase II is characterized by the individual starting to drink secretly, feeling guilty, becoming defensive about drinking, with rapid gulping of drinks producing further blackouts and excessive use of denial and rationalization

stem: Phase III of alcohol dependence is known as the Crucial Phase. Which statement MOST accurately describes the defining feature of this phase that is described as loss of control?
options: Loss of control is described as the inability to choose whether or not to drink; there is binge drinking lasting from a few hours to several weeks; drinking is the total focus; the individual risks losing everything including job, marriage, family, friends, and self-respect
Loss of control is described as the onset of blackouts and amnesia immediately following alcohol consumption; the individual starts drinking secretly and uses denial and rationalization excessively
Loss of control is described as severe physical and psychological consequences including liver damage, cardiovascular problems, neurological impairments, and mental health disorders in the end stage

stem: Phase IV of alcohol dependence is the Chronic Phase. Which consequences specifically characterise this end-stage phase?
options: Severe physical and psychological consequences; health significantly deteriorates leading to liver damage (cirrhosis), cardiovascular problems, neurological impairments, and mental health disorders; profound disruptions in relationships, employment, and overall functioning

Intense cravings and inability to choose whether or not to drink; binge drinking lasting hours to weeks; loss of job, marriage, family, friends, and self-respect; withdrawal symptoms on attempting to quit
Blackouts and brief periods of amnesia during drinking; alcohol becomes a drug required rather than a source of pleasure; individual begins drinking secretly and feels guilty about consumption

stem: According to the text, what is the street name for Heroin in Nigeria?
options: Smack, junk, H, dope, horse, Brown Sugar
Meth, ice, crystal, speed, crank, mkpuru-mmiri
Coke, blow, snow, crack, rock

stem: The street names for Cannabis listed in the text include which of the following groups?
options: Marijuana, Weed, pot, grass, ganja, India herm: Igbo, Kush, Skunk, Loud, Mary Jane
Smack, junk, H, dope, horse, Brown Sugar
Spice, K2, synthetic marijuana, fake weed, herbal incense

stem: Methamphetamine has a specific Nigerian street name listed in the text. Which of the following CORRECTLY identifies its street names?
options: Meth, ice, crystal, speed, crank, mkpuru-mmiri
Coke, blow, snow, crack, rock
Smack, junk, H, dope, horse, Brown Sugar

stem: Ketamine is listed as a substance of abuse. Which street names are SPECIFICALLY associated with Ketamine in the text?
options: Special K, vitamin K, cat Valium, horse tranquilizer
Shrooms, magic mushrooms, caps, zoomers
Benzos, tranks, downers, sleeping pills, candy

stem: Psilocybin mushrooms are listed as a substance of abuse. Which street names are SPECIFICALLY associated with them in the text?
options: Shrooms, magic mushrooms, caps, zoomers
Special K, vitamin K, cat Valium, horse tranquilizer
Spice, K2, synthetic marijuana, fake weed, herbal incense

stem: Inhalants are listed as substances of abuse. Which street names are associated with Inhalants in the text?
options: Whippets, poppers, rush, huff, laughing gas
Shrooms, magic mushrooms, caps, zoomers
Glue, petrol, High on gas, sniff, solvent

stem: Alcohol street names in Nigeria include which of the following as listed in the text?
options: Booze, brew, hooch, juice, sauce, Paraga, OG, Kai Kai, Alomo, Sepe
Marijuana, Weed, pot, grass, ganja, India herm: Igbo, Kush, Skunk, Loud, Mary Jane
Meth, ice, crystal, speed, crank, mkpuru-mmiri

stem: Co-occurring mental health disorders are described as a factor contributing to the development of substance abuse. Which mental health conditions are SPECIFICALLY listed as examples in the text?
options: Depression, anxiety, post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), or attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) â individuals may turn to substances as a way to self-medicate or cope with their emotional struggles
Schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and obsessive compulsive disorder â conditions in which substances are used to suppress hallucinations and intrusive thoughts
Borderline personality disorder, antisocial personality disorder, and major depressive disorder â conditions that share neurobiological pathways with substance use disorders

stem: Detoxification is listed as a component of substance abuse management. Which statement MOST accurately describes what detoxification involves?
options: For individuals with severe substance dependence, medical detoxification may be necessary to safely manage withdrawal symptoms; it is typically conducted under medical supervision to ensure the individual's safety and comfort during withdrawal

Detoxification involves the administration of behavioural therapies including cognitive-behavioural therapy and motivational interviewing to manage withdrawal symptoms without pharmacological intervention

Detoxification is conducted in outpatient settings for all levels of substance dependence and involves the use of support groups and peer counselling to assist individuals through the withdrawal process

stem: Behavioural therapies for substance abuse management are described in the text. Which of the following CORRECTLY identifies the specific therapies named?
options: Cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT), motivational interviewing (MI), and contingency management â helping individuals understand underlying factors, develop coping skills, modify unhealthy thought patterns, and make positive behavioural changes
Dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT), psychodynamic therapy, and interpersonal therapy â helping individuals explore unresolved conflicts and emotional dysregulation contributing to substance use
Family systems therapy, acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT), and narrative therapy â helping individuals reconstruct their personal narratives to separate identity from substance use patterns

stem: Medication-Assisted Treatment (MAT) is described in the text. Which medications are SPECIFICALLY named for opioid dependence and alcohol dependence respectively?
options: Opioids: methadone, buprenorphine; Alcohol: naltrexone, acamprosate â MAT is often combined with counseling and behavioural therapies for optimal outcomes
Opioids: naltrexone, acamprosate; Alcohol: methadone, buprenorphine â MAT is used independently of psychotherapy for best pharmacological outcomes
Opioids: diazepam, haloperidol; Alcohol: chlorpromazine, paraldehyde â chemical restraint agents repurposed for managing substance withdrawal symptoms

stem: Support groups are listed as a management strategy for substance abuse. Which specific support groups are named in the text?
options: Alcoholics Anonymous (AA) and Narcotics Anonymous (NA) â providing peer support, encouragement, and guidance from others who have experienced similar struggles
Substance Abusers Anonymous (SAA) and Recovering Addicts Network (RAN) â providing community-based peer support for all substances
Dual Recovery Anonymous (DRA) and Cocaine Anonymous (CA) â providing dual diagnosis-specific peer support for substance and mental health comorbidity

stem: Dual Diagnosis Treatment is described as a management strategy for substance abuse. Which statement MOST accurately describes what it involves?
options: Many individuals with substance abuse disorders also have co-occurring mental health disorders; integrated treatment that addresses both the substance abuse and mental health conditions is crucial for successful recovery, involving collaboration between addiction specialists and mental health professionals

Dual Diagnosis Treatment involves the simultaneous prescription of two different medications â one for the substance use disorder and one for the co-occurring mental health condition â without the need for psychotherapy

Dual Diagnosis Treatment is reserved for individuals with severe substance use disorder (6 or more DSM-5 criteria) who also meet criteria for a psychotic disorder such as schizophrenia or bipolar disorder

stem: Restraint is defined in the text. Which definition MOST accurately describes what restraint is?
options: The use of appliances and chemical agent to impede the movement of the body of a restless and aggressive patient in order to prevent harming self and others, thereby providing temporal external control for a patient who cannot provide their own internal control
The use of padded rooms and seclusion to confine a patient who is unable to leave, with walls padded and kept free of items that could cause injury to promote comfort and safety
The use of hypnotic drugs and tranquilizers to sedate and calm an aggressive patient, thereby preventing injury to self and others through chemical means

stem: Physical Restraint is distinguished from Chemical Restraint and Seclusion in the text. Which definition MOST precisely describes physical restraint?
options: Devices, instruments, or appliances used to limit specific parts of the patient's body such as arms or legs, thus impeding the movement of a violent, aggressive, or restless patient â e.g. leather belt or vest may be used to stop the patient from getting out of bed
The use of hypnotic drugs and tranquilizers to sedate and calm an aggressive patient, thereby preventing injury to self and others
A type of restraint where a patient is confined in a room by himself and is unable to leave; the room is locked, walls are padded, and kept free of items that could cause injury

stem: Seclusion is described in the text as a type of restraint. Which statement MOST accurately defines seclusion?
options: A type of restraint in which a patient is confirmed in a room by himself and he is unable to leave the room; the room is locked, the walls are padded and kept free of items that could cause injury, and arranged with minimal items to promote the patient's comfort and safety
Devices, instruments, or appliances used to limit specific parts of the patient's body to impede movement of a violent, aggressive, or restless patient
The use of appliances and chemical agents to impede the movement of a restless and aggressive patient to prevent harming self and others

stem: Which of the following are listed as ADVANTAGES of Restraint in the text?
options: It prevents patient from inflicting injury to self; prevents inflicting injury to others and damage to properties; enhances close physical observation and assessment of patient; prevents distraction in routine nursing management of other patients; chemical restraints produce therapeutic effect on the patient due to the tranquilizing properties; prevents aimless wandering and prevents suicide

It prevents patient from inflicting injury to self; prevents legal implications of uncontrolled aggressive behaviour; provides opportunity for the patient to reflect on behaviour; reduces the need for emergency medication administration

It prevents patient from inflicting injury to self; prevents patient from eloping from the ward; ensures medication compliance; reduces nursing workload; allows for uninterrupted therapeutic activities with other patients

stem: Which of the following are listed as DISADVANTAGES of Restraint in the text?
options: High risk of injury such as physical restraints being too tight causing skin injury or necrosis due to blockage of blood flow; extensive injury and trauma from struggling; physical restraint is humanly debasing; negative psychological effect on the patient; chemical restraint of sedatives and narcoleptics can produce toxic effects

High risk of injury from falling out of bed; increased agitation when restrained; risk of respiratory depression from chemical agents; risk of developing pressure sores from immobility; negative effect on nurse-patient therapeutic relationship

High risk of fracture from mechanical restraint devices; increased risk of aspiration pneumonia in chemically restrained patients; legal liability for the institution; patient rights violations; prolonged hospitalisation

stem: The nurse's responsibilities to a patient on PHYSICAL restraint include a requirement about the number of persons needed for restraint. What does the text specify?
options: Preferable 5 or a minimum of 4 persons should be used to restrain the patient
Preferable 4 or a minimum of 3 persons should be used to restrain the patient
Preferable 6 or a minimum of 5 persons should be used to restrain the patient

stem: Regarding the proper positioning of a physically restrained patient, which of the following is stated in the text?
options: Patient should be restrained with legs spread and one arm restrained to one side and the other arm restrained over the head
Patient should be restrained with both arms at the sides of the body and legs together to maintain neutral anatomical alignment
Patient should be restrained in a semi-recumbent position with head elevated at 30 degrees to prevent aspiration

stem: The text specifies where pressure should NEVER be applied during physical restraint. Which body areas are specifically prohibited?
options: Pressure should never be applied to the neck, chest, or abdomen as this can severely limit respiration or cause injury to vulnerable areas of the body; bony prominences should be padded to maintain good body alignment
Pressure should never be applied to the head, face, or genitals as these are the most vulnerable areas; all restraint devices should be cushioned with foam padding
Pressure should never be applied to the wrists, ankles, or knees as these joints are vulnerable to fracture; restraint should focus on the trunk and proximal limbs only

stem: Regarding restraint at major joints when arms or legs are held, what does the text specify?
options: If arms or legs are held, this should be close to major joints to avoid the risk of fracture; restrain should be placed so that intravenous fluid can be given when necessary
If arms or legs are held, this should be as far from major joints as possible to prevent dislocation; padding should be applied at all contact points with the restraint device
If arms or legs are held, restraint devices should be applied between major joints at the mid-shaft of long bones to distribute pressure evenly and minimise neurovascular compromise

stem: The ethical consideration of restraint specifies time limits. In an emergency, restraint or seclusion may be initiated without a physician's order, but the physician must be notified within what timeframe?
options: As soon as possible but not longer than one hour after the initiation of restraint or seclusion
As soon as possible but not longer than two hours after the initiation of restraint or seclusion
As soon as possible but not longer than thirty minutes after the initiation of restraint or seclusion

stem: The ethical consideration of restraint states that the patient's restraint must be observed and assessed over what interval, and with regard to which specific parameters?
options: Observed and assessed over 10-15 minutes with regards to psychological condition; circulation, respiration, nutrition, hydration, and elimination; such assessment should be documented in the patient's record chart or folder
Observed and assessed over 30-minute intervals with regards to vital signs, level of consciousness, skin integrity, and patient's behavioural response to restraint
Observed and assessed hourly with regards to the patient's physical and psychological condition; all observations must be countersigned by the physician within 24 hours

stem: The nurse's responsibilities during CHEMICAL restraint include which of the following listed in the text?
options: Assess and ensure prescribed drugs are administered and monitored; assess level of consciousness; monitor side effects of drugs used; observe vital signs to ensure they are within normal range; ensure emergency drugs and equipment are readily available in case of adverse effect; carry out full medical and neurological assessment and institute appropriate treatment

Administer prescribed drugs only when the patient is physically restrained; monitor the patient's consciousness hourly; document the specific drug and dose used; notify the physician if the patient develops respiratory depression

Ensure prescribed drugs are given orally rather than parenterally where possible; monitor the patient's gait and coordination; document the time of drug administration and the patient's behavioural response

stem: Which of the following drugs are listed as CHEMICAL RESTRAINT agents in the text?
options: Diazepam, Chlorpromazine, Paraldehyde, Haloperidol (used in conjunction with other tranquilizers), most benzodiazepines, hypnotics, and several psychotropic medications
Methadone, Buprenorphine, Naltrexone, Acamprosate, and Diazepam used in combination for chemical restraint and addiction management
Haloperidol, Risperidone, Olanzapine, Clozapine, and Quetiapine â second-generation antipsychotics used as the primary chemical restraint agents

stem: The text warns about a specific risk with sedatives like benzodiazepine or barbiturates used as chemical restraint. What is this risk?
options: Sedatives like benzodiazepine or barbiturates can cause apnoea especially in patients who have taken other drugs; there is also the possibility of legal implication in cases of injury on the patient
Sedatives like benzodiazepine or barbiturates can cause paradoxical agitation in some patients, making the aggressive behaviour worse rather than calming the patient
Sedatives like benzodiazepine or barbiturates can cause excessive sedation leading to aspiration pneumonia, especially in patients who have not been adequately assessed for gag reflex

stem: The leather restraint is specifically mentioned as the safest type of restraint. Which statement from the text CORRECTLY describes this?
options: Leather restraints are the safest type of restraint to be used
Leather restraints are preferred over fabric restraints because they allow for better monitoring of skin integrity
Leather restraints are the most humane form of physical restraint and should always be applied by at least three staff members

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 24-year-old man is admitted to the psychiatric ward with violent, aggressive behaviour. The nurse prepares to apply physical restraint. Which action by the nurse is MOST consistent with the nurse's responsibilities for physical restraint?
options: Ensure that a minimum of 4 persons (preferable 5) are available; use leather restraints as the safest type; explain to the patient the reasons for restraint and that his cooperation is needed; pad bony prominences; restrain with legs spread and one arm to one side and the other arm over the head

Ensure that a minimum of 3 persons are available; apply padded restraints to wrists and ankles simultaneously; apply pressure to the chest to prevent the patient from rising; document the restraint after it is applied

Ensure that a minimum of 2 persons are available; apply restraints without explanation to prevent the patient from resisting; apply pressure near the neck to prevent head-butting; check restraints every hour

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 35-year-old woman with alcohol use disorder is admitted in Phase III (Crucial Phase). Which presentation is MOST consistent with Phase III as described in the text?
options: She is unable to choose whether or not to drink; she has been on a binge lasting several weeks; drinking is her total focus; she has lost her job, marriage, and self-respect; she experiences withdrawal symptoms when she tries to stop
She starts drinking secretly and feels guilty; she gulps drinks rapidly and has blackouts; she uses excessive denial and rationalisation; she is preoccupied with maintaining her supply

She experiences severe physical deterioration with liver cirrhosis, cardiovascular problems, and neurological impairments; her life is profoundly disrupted in relationships, employment, and overall functioning

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A patient is placed in a padded room with the door locked after displaying extreme aggression that could not be managed with less restrictive measures. Which type of restraint is being used?
options: Seclusion (environmental restraint) â a type of restraint in which a patient is confirmed in a room by himself, unable to leave, with walls padded and kept free of items that could cause injury
Physical restraint â using devices and appliances to limit specific parts of the patient's body such as arms or legs to impede movement
Chemical restraint â involving hypnotic drugs and tranquilizers to sedate and calm an aggressive patient thereby preventing injury to self and others

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nurse discovers a patient on physical restraint has developed a reddened area at the wrist where the restraint is applied. The skin is intact but the area is cool and discoloured. Which nursing responsibility was MOST likely neglected?
options: Observation of vital signs and monitoring the patient's skin for injury or blood flow problems under the restraints; restraint should be checked periodically for safety
Ensuring the patient's physical comfort was monitored closely and that the restraint was placed close to major joints to avoid fracture risk
Documenting the reason for the restraint and the patient's response to treatment while on restraint in the patient's case file

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A recovering alcoholic patient in Phase IV presents with jaundice, confusion, and tremors. The nurse notes the patient has liver damage and neurological impairments. Which phase of alcohol dependence does this MOST accurately represent?
options: Phase IV: The Chronic Phase â where individuals experience severe physical and psychological consequences; health significantly deteriorates leading to liver damage (cirrhosis), cardiovascular problems, neurological impairments, and mental health disorders
Phase III: The Crucial Phase â where individuals experience intense cravings, binge drinking, loss of control, and withdrawal symptoms when attempting to quit
Phase II: The Early Alcohol Phase â where blackouts begin and alcohol is no longer a source of pleasure but a drug required by the individual

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A 19-year-old student confesses to using a substance that his friends call 'mkpuru-mmiri.' The nurse recognises this as the Nigerian street name for which substance?
options: Methamphetamine â with street names Meth, ice, crystal, speed, crank, mkpuru-mmiri
Heroin â with street names Smack, junk, H, dope, horse, Brown Sugar
Cannabis â with street names Marijuana, Weed, pot, grass, ganja, India herm: Igbo, Kush, Skunk, Loud, Mary Jane

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nurse is reviewing the management plan of a patient with both alcohol use disorder and major depressive disorder. Which management strategy is MOST appropriate for this clinical situation?
options: Dual diagnosis treatment â integrated treatment that addresses both the substance abuse and mental health conditions simultaneously, involving collaboration between addiction specialists and mental health professionals
Detoxification followed by medication-assisted treatment with naltrexone and acamprosate for the alcohol use disorder only, deferring treatment of the depression until sobriety is established
Behavioural therapies only â cognitive-behavioural therapy and motivational interviewing without pharmacological intervention, as medications may interact with the patient's residual alcohol

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nurse is called to handle a violent patient and administers haloperidol as ordered. After 30 minutes, the patient develops respiratory distress. Which nursing responsibility for chemical restraint was MOST critical to prevent this outcome?
options: Observation of vital signs to ensure they are within normal range; ensuring emergency drugs and equipment are readily available in case of any adverse effect; noting that sedatives can cause apnoea especially in patients who have taken other drugs
Documenting the name and dose of the chemical restraint used before administration; ensuring a physician's order was obtained within one hour of initiating chemical restraint
Assessing the patient's level of consciousness before administering the drug; ensuring the patient was physically restrained before any chemical agent was given

stem: CASE SCENARIO: A nurse is counselling a patient in recovery about relapse prevention. According to the text, which of the following MOST accurately describes the relapse prevention strategy?
options: Developing relapse prevention strategies, identifying triggers and high-risk situations, learning healthy coping mechanisms for maintaining long-term sobriety, and continuing support through counselling, support groups, and aftercare programs
Abstaining completely from all social situations where substances may be present; attending daily Alcoholics Anonymous meetings and reporting any cravings to the nurse immediately
Using medication-assisted treatment as the sole relapse prevention strategy; methadone or buprenorphine completely eliminates the risk of relapse without the need for additional psychosocial support

stem: Substance abuse is characterized by the inability to ____________ or stop substance use despite the ____________ consequences it causes.
To establish a diagnosis of substance use disorder, the DSM-5 requires the individual to meet at least ____________ or more of the ____________ outlined criteria within a ____________-month period.
The severity of substance use disorder is classified as: Mild if ____________ criteria are met; Moderate if ____________ criteria are met; Severe if ____________ or more criteria are met.

Psychological dependence involves intense ____________ and desire for the drug, while physical dependence is marked by the occurrence of ____________ symptoms when the substance is reduced or discontinued.

Phase I of alcohol dependence is called the ____________ Phase. It is characterized by the use of alcohol to relieve everyday ____________ and tensions of life, during which ____________ develops and the amount required to achieve the desired effect increases.

Phase II of alcohol dependence begins with ____________ â brief periods of ____________ that occur during or immediately following a period of taking the alcohol. Now the alcohol is no longer a source of ____________ or relief for the individual.

Phase III of alcohol dependence is known as the ____________ Phase. Loss of control is described as the inability to ____________ whether or not to drink. There is ____________ drinking lasting from a few hours to several weeks.
Phase IV of alcohol dependence is known as the ____________ Phase, characterised by liver damage (____________), cardiovascular problems, neurological impairments, and mental health disorders.

Restraint is defined as the use of ____________ and chemical agent to impede the movement of the body of a restless and aggressive patient in order to prevent harming ____________ and others, thereby providing ____________ external control.
The three types of restraint are: (1) ____________ restraint (mechanical restraint), (2) ____________ restraint, and (3) ____________ (environmental restraint).
For physical restraint, it is preferable to use ____________ persons or a minimum of ____________ persons to restrain the patient. ____________ restraints are the safest type of restraint to be used.
Pressure should never be applied to the ____________, ____________, or abdomen as this can severely limit ____________ or cause injury to vulnerable areas of the body.

Sedatives like ____________ or barbiturates can cause ____________ especially in patients who have taken other drugs; there is also a possibility of ____________ implication in cases of injury on the patient.

In ethical consideration of restraint, the physician must be notified as soon as possible but not longer than ____________ hour after the initiation of restraint or seclusion. The patient's restraint must be observed and assessed over ____________ minutes.
Chemical restraint agents listed in the text include: ____________, Chlorpromazine, ____________, Haloperidol, most benzodiazepines, hypnotics, and several ____________ medications.

Medication-Assisted Treatment (MAT) for opioid dependence includes ____________ and buprenorphine; for alcohol dependence it includes ____________ and acamprosate. MAT is often combined with ____________ and behavioural therapies for optimal outcomes.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A patient on chemical restraint develops shallow breathing 20 minutes after receiving haloperidol and diazepam. The nurse recalls that sedatives can cause ____________ especially in patients who have taken other drugs. The nurse should immediately ensure that ____________ drugs and equipment are readily available and carry out a full ____________ and neurological assessment.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A 40-year-old woman in Phase III of alcohol dependence is admitted. The nurse documents that she has experienced loss of ____________, describing it as the inability to choose whether or not to ____________. She has lost her job and marriage. The nurse identifies this as consistent with the ____________ Phase.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A 28-year-old man uses a substance his friends call 'mkpuru-mmiri.' The nurse identifies this as the Nigerian street name for ____________. This substance is classified under the category of ____________ in the DSM-5, and long-term use has been linked to ____________, depression, and paranoia.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A physically restrained patient develops a cool, discoloured area at the wrist. The nurse identifies this as due to ____________ of blood flow. The nurse should check that the restraint is not too ____________ and should observe the patient's skin for ____________ under the restraints.

CASE SCENARIO (Fill-in): A psychiatric nurse is managing a patient with both substance use disorder and PTSD. She coordinates with the addiction specialist and mental health team, providing integrated treatment â this is called ____________ diagnosis treatment. The management addresses the ____________, psychological, and ____________ aspects of addiction comprehensively.
